lesson.menu_desc: Conditional Variations for Bargaining (Were we to commit to a larger volume...; provided that delivery is on time) -- Precision and Professional Pushback -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 19 -- Negotiating Contracts and Terms -- Conditional Variations (Should the supplier miss the deadline, the penalty clause applies)
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Fabiana Michelly Silva | Aula 19 -- Negotiating Contracts and Terms

listenings.text: Thank you both for joining the contract review. Let me be precise about where we stand and where we can move. On price, we are close, but I want to be clear on the terms. Provided that you commit to the renegotiated freight volume, we can hold the rate for the full term. On delivery, the penalty clause is non-negotiable: should the supplier miss a confirmed deadline, the agreed penalty applies, with no waiver. That said, we are reasonable. Were you to bring the lead time down to ten days, we would drop the penalty rate by half, as a sign of good faith. On liability, we need a cap. Unless we agree a liability cap of two million per incident, I cannot recommend this to the board. The force majeure clause stays standard, but I want the termination terms tightened: either party may terminate on ninety days notice, on condition that all outstanding deliverables are settled first. So, to be concrete, here is our counter-offer: hold the rate, halve the penalty for a ten-day lead time, cap liability at two million, and tighten termination. If those four points are acceptable, we have a deal, and I will send the amendment for signature today.

Hi Fabiana, it is Marcus, calling back about the contract negotiation. I have read your counter-offer and, on the whole, it is sharp and well-judged. A few notes before you send the amendment. First, I fully support the liability cap; unless we have a cap of two million per incident, we should not sign, so hold firm on that. Second, the penalty clause: I like that it is non-negotiable, but make sure the wording is precise, because should there be any ambiguity, the supplier will exploit it. Third, on the lead-time concession, halving the penalty for a ten-day lead time is a fair trade, provided that it is genuinely binding and not just a verbal understanding. One thing to push on: the termination terms. Ninety days notice is reasonable, but add that termination only takes effect on condition that all outstanding deliverables are settled, otherwise we carry the risk. Finally, keep the force majeure clause standard, do not over-engineer it. If they accept those four points in good faith, we have a strong, balanced contract. Send me the final draft and I will get sign-off. Good work, talk soon.

extra_audio.text: Fabiana opens the contract review and sets out her position on price and terms. She offers to hold the rate, provided that the supplier commits to the renegotiated freight volume. She then makes the penalty clause non-negotiable: should the supplier miss a deadline, the penalty applies. As a concession, she offers to halve the penalty were the supplier to bring the lead time down to ten days. She insists on a liability cap, on condition that termination settles all outstanding deliverables, and closes by summarizing the four-point counter-offer for signature.
